Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd just kind of do things when I felt like it, which wasn't super often. Sometimes I'd binge-watch lecture videos for hours, other times I'd go weeks without touching anything. I learn better by doing stuff with my hands anyway, so sitting and reading or watching videos feels tedious to me. I'd usually wait until something absolutely needed to get done before I'd actually sit down and focus.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I definitely wasn't one of those people who studied at the same time every day or anything like that. If there was a deadline coming up, I'd cram, but otherwise I'd just let things slide. With twelve online courses, it's hard to keep track of everything anyway, so I'd just deal with whatever was most urgent at the moment.
Q3: Usually it was either boredom or panic that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd be scrolling on my phone with nothing to do and figure I might as well check the LMS. Other times it was that sinking feeling when I realized something was probably due soon. I logged in pretty frequently, like thirty times, but a lot of those were just quick check-ins where I didn't actually do much.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. It never became a habit or anything natural for me. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more, especially when I was procrastinating from other things, but it wasn't consistent. The only routine I had was checking if there were any urgent notifications, and even that was sporadic.
Q5: The videos were actually pretty useful when I watched them. I clocked a lot of hours on those because I could just let them play while I was doing other things or actually pay attention when the topic was interesting. The peer interactions were okay too because talking to other people made things feel less boring. At least with those, I felt like something was actually happening.
Q6: Discussion posts and a lot of the reading materials I basically ignored. I only made like three posts the whole time because typing out responses felt like such a chore. I'd also skip over anything that looked too dense or theoretical because I knew I'd just zone out anyway. If it didn't seem immediately relevant to passing, I probably didn't bother with it.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to an exam or assignment, then it felt worth doing. Also, anything that broke up the monotony or involved actually doing something rather than just reading. I'm a hands-on learner, so activities that let me apply concepts or interact with something felt more valuable than just absorbing information passively.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, my brain just filed it under 'ignore.' Required activities at least had the threat of consequences attached, so I'd drag myself through them. Optional ones felt like extra work for no real payoff, especially when I was already bored with the required stuff.
Q9: Definitely. Once I downloaded lecture slides or materials, I'd study from those offline. I don't have reliable internet access at home, so I'd grab what I needed when I was somewhere with wifi and then work from the files later. A lot of my actual studying happened with printed notes or downloaded PDFs that the LMS would never know about.
Q10: There were tons of times like that. I'd download a bunch of videos and watch them on my phone during commutes or while waiting around. Or I'd study from notes I'd written out by hand because that's how I actually learn things. The LMS only saw me download stuff once, but I'd review those materials multiple times offline.
Q11: All the time. I'd access something once to download it, and then that was the last the LMS heard from me on that material. But I'd actually spend hours going through it offline, especially the videos. It's just more practical for me given my internet situation, and honestly I prefer studying without being connected anyway.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just couldn't bring myself to care. The course material felt repetitive or just boring, and I'd find myself staring at the screen without actually absorbing anything. Sometimes it was just general life stuff getting in the way, but mostly it was that feeling of 'why does this matter' creeping in.
Q13: With limited resources and twelve courses to juggle, yeah, I had to make choices about what got my attention. When I was tired or overwhelmed, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but sometimes later never came. Competing responsibilities definitely meant some things just didn't get done.
Q14: Oh, for sure. I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials, but my mind was completely elsewhere. Sometimes I'd watch a whole video and realize I'd absorbed nothing because I was too bored or distracted. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning anything.
Q15: Constantly. I'd put off assignments even when I knew they were important because the motivation just wasn't there. It's like I knew I should do them, but the actual doing part felt impossible. I'd tell myself I work better under pressure anyway, which is partially true but also just an excuse I used a lot.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. Without that pressure, I'd just drift along watching videos occasionally but not producing anything. When a deadline was close, I'd suddenly become super active on the LMS, cramming and submitting things at the last minute. It wasn't healthy but it worked.
Q17: Not really, to be honest. By the time feedback came, I'd already mentally moved on to the next thing. I'd glance at it, maybe feel briefly good or bad about it, and then forget about it. It didn't really change how I approached the next assignment or anything like that.
Q18: The structure was pretty loose, which worked for and against me. On one hand, I liked not being micromanaged. On the other hand, without constant structure pushing me along, I'd just float and not engage much. Having more flexibility meant I could procrastinate more easily, which I definitely did.
Q19: Having everything online made it easy to access but also easy to ignore. There wasn't enough hands-on or interactive stuff for someone like me who learns by doing. The discussion forums felt forced and boring. More practical activities or things that required active participation would have helped me stay engaged instead of just passively watching videos.
Q20: I think that's exactly what I did sometimes. I'd be logged in, watching videos, clicking around, but not actually completing the assignments. It could be procrastination, or feeling overwhelmed by the actual work, or just engaging with the easy stuff while avoiding the hard stuff. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That's basically my story. My attendance and assignment completion were terrible, but I still got a distinction because I knew the material when it counted. I studied a lot offline and I'm good at exams when I actually focus. The LMS doesn't capture that I can cram effectively and perform well on tests even without doing all the busywork.
Q22: Logs can show when you're online and what you click on, but they can't show if you're actually paying attention or learning. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking you do away from the screen, and whether you're genuinely engaged or just going through the motions. They capture behavior but not understanding.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just do it. It's about being mentally present, not just physically logged in. I wasn't always engaged even when I was active on the LMS, and sometimes I was deeply engaged with course material without the LMS knowing at all.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned from downloaded materials offline, or that I actually found some topics interesting even though I seemed disengaged. It also wouldn't show that I was dealing with limited resources and no internet at home, which affected how I used the platform. My exam scores show I learned the material, even if my participation looked bad.
